front: Film: Marion Crane is repeatedly stabbed in the shower to a shrieking Bernard Herrmann score; a character then yells 'Mother! Oh God, Mother! Blood!'
Film: Norman Bates tells a woman 'a boy's best friend is his mother' and 'we all go a little mad sometimes'; he stuffs birds and has a split personality triggered by sexual attraction
Film: Slavoj Zizek's The Pervert's Guide to the Cinema maps the three floors of the antagonist's mansion onto superego, ego, and id
Film: the acrophobic Scottie (Jimmy Stewart) follows a woman played by Kim Novak; she falls from a bell tower after seeing a nun
Film: Chris Marker's Sans Soleil calls this the only film to depict 'impossible memory,' revisiting its San Francisco locations and recreating its POV driving shots
Film: first use of the dolly zoom technique, now nicknamed the 'Vertigo effect'
Film: Laura Mulvey's 'Visual Pleasure and Narrative Cinema' analyzes the male hero's scopophilic eroticism in this film alongside Rear Window and Marnie
Film: climax has the protagonist use camera flashbulbs to temporarily blind the murderous antagonist Lars Thorwald
Film: famous crop duster scene near Bus Stop 41; described as 'the first Bond film'
Film: climactic scene has Cary Grant and Eva Marie Saint hanging from a ledge on the face of Mount Rushmore
Film: the lead actress (Tippi Hedren) was badly bruised by real bird claw marks during filming of the climactic scene
Director: Francois Truffaut conducted an eight-day interview (with Helen Scott as interpreter) published in 1966, cementing his reputation as an auteur
Director: Laura Mulvey's 'Visual Pleasure and Narrative Cinema' focuses on him alongside Josef von Sternberg
Director: Eric Rohmer and Claude Chabrol wrote an extensive study positing his 'Catholic guilt transference'
Director: in the Truffaut interview, called drama 'life with the dull bits cut out' and explained the MacGuffin concept
Director: as a child was briefly locked up at a police station on his father's instruction; later said 'I'm not against the police; I'm just afraid of them'
Director: TV show Alfred Hitchcock Presents began each episode with him walking into the silhouette of his profile to 'Funeral March for a Marionette'
